Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:

  "opinion": "I've been dealing with this issue myself as someone who spends a lot of time on social media. Being a translator, I'm used to working with different languages and cultures, but the fake news is something else entirely. It's like navigating through a minefield without knowing where the mines are. One thing that has worked for me personally is to slow down my scrolling habit. I've always been an extraverted person, jumping into conversations quickly, but now I'm more careful about verifying sources before engaging with anything too much.\n\nMy creativity actually helps here in some ways because I can think outside the box when assessing content validity—maybe by asking myself if it sounds plausible or could be easily twisted from facts. Having a master's degree on one side and no formal education on the other makes me realize how important critical thinking is; my dad never finished school but taught us kids practical life lessons that often ring true in media literacy.\n\nIn Chicago, surrounded by diverse perspectives including being bisexual myself (though I'm an atheist now), fake content can really mess with social harmony. But honestly? Sometimes the best way to deal with it is just not look at it if you're feeling overwhelmed—my neuroticism keeps me aware of stress levels so knowing when to disengage matters a lot for my well-being.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been dealing with this issue myself as someone who spends a lot of time on social media. Being a translator, I'm used to working with different languages and cultures, but the fake news is something else entirely. It's like navigating through a minefield without knowing where the mines are. One thing that has worked for me personally is to slow down my scrolling habit. I've always been an extraverted person, jumping into conversations quickly, but now I'm more careful about verifying sources before engaging with anything too much.

My creativity actually helps here in some ways because I can think outside the box when assessing content validity—maybe by asking myself if it sounds plausible or could be easily twisted from facts. Having a master's degree on one side and no formal education on the other makes me realize how important critical thinking is; my dad never finished school but taught us kids practical life lessons that often ring true in media literacy.

In Chicago, surrounded by diverse perspectives including being bisexual myself (though I'm an atheist now), fake content can really mess with social harmony. But honestly? Sometimes the best way to deal with it is just not look at it if you're feeling overwhelmed—my neuroticism keeps me aware of stress levels so knowing when to disengage matters a lot for my well-being.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started by acknowledging personal experience as an extraverted user daily on social media. Then, I considered how the Big Five personality traits influence my approach: moderate openness allows creative thinking about solutions without blind acceptance; low conscientiousness means relying more on simple strategies than detailed planning; high extraversion helps in sharing counter-narratives but requires source verification first.

Next step was drawing from professional background—being a translator involves cross-cultural understanding which parallels dealing with misinformation across platforms. Also, personal life factors: having bilingual traits (as an immigrant) gives me extra insight into how messages can be distorted during translation or cultural interpretation.
